content.problemStatement:
Chef is planning a grand cooking tournament for aspiring chefs in the region.
The competition progresses with a number of one-on-one elimination rounds until only one chef remains.
For each round, two chefs are chosen at random for a cook-off.
The loser of the cook-off is elimited, while the winner moves on.
Additionally, each chef begins with a positive number of prize tokens
(some chefs begin with more than others, based on their performance in a qualification round).
The winner of each cook-off receives all of the loser's prize tokens, and the loser is
awarded a prize based on the number of prize tokens he/she had accrued.

The final cook-off always receives the most publicity.
Your task it to compute the expected difference between the number of prize tokens of the final two
competitors before the final cook-off.
You will be given the number of tokens each chef begins with.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case is given on a single line. The line starts with an integer C (2 ≤ C ≤ 100) – the number of chefs – followed by C positive integers a1 … aC, where ai is the initial number of prize tokens of the i‑th chef. The sum of all ai in a test case does not exceed 100.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the expected absolute difference between the numbers of tokens of the two chefs that reach the final round. The answer must be printed with exactly 6 digits after the decimal point.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the only possible final two competitors are the two chefs with 1 token each, so the expected difference is 0. For the second test case, the possible final two competitors are the chef with 2 tokens and one of the chefs with 1 token, so the expected difference is 1. For the third test case, the possible final two competitors are the chef with 30 tokens and one of the chefs with 10 tokens, so the expected difference is 20.

solution.approach:
The process can be described as follows: at any moment we have a multiset of token values. One unordered pair of chefs is chosen uniformly at random. The chef with the larger token count wins and his new token count becomes the sum of the two participants; the loser disappears. If the two values are equal the winner can be chosen arbitrarily – the resulting multiset is the same (the sum of the two equal numbers).

Because the winner is always the larger value, the largest token present in a state can never be eliminated; it only grows when it meets another chef. Consequently the whole process is completely determined by the order in which pairs are selected.

We compute the expected difference by a recursive DP over all possible multisets. For a state S (sorted list of token values) with |S| = n:

* If n == 2 the answer for this state is |S[0] - S[1]|.
* Otherwise every unordered pair (i, j) (i < j) is chosen with probability 2 / (n·(n‑1)). Let x = S[i], y = S[j] (x ≤ y). The new state S' is obtained by removing both elements and inserting y + x. The expected value for S is the average of the expected values of all S'.

The total sum of tokens never exceeds 100, therefore the number of distinct multisets (partitions of numbers up to 100) is below 200 000. We memoise the result for each multiset, which makes the algorithm fast enough for the given limits.

State representation: we store a frequency array cnt[0…100] (cnt[v] = how many chefs currently have exactly v tokens). The array is encoded as a string of 101 bytes and used as a key in an unordered_map (or HashMap) for memoisation.

Complexities: For a state with n chefs we examine O(n²) pairs. The total number of reachable states is bounded by the number of integer partitions of ≤100, i.e. ≈2·10⁵. Hence the overall time is well within a few seconds. Memory consumption is O(number of states) ≈ a few MB.
solution.timeComplexity: O(number_of_states · average_n²) ≤ O(2·10⁵ · 100) ≈ 2·10⁷ operations in the worst case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(number_of_states) for memoisation, roughly a few megabytes.
